parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded internal tank. This is evidenced by the rusty brown water visible in the drip tray and the visible rust streaks around the pipe connections in the photos. The rust indicates that the internal lining of the geyser tank has failed, allowing the steel tank to rust.

This issue typically develops when the sacrificial anode rod inside the geyser tank has been fully consumed. The anode is designed to corrode instead of the tank, protecting the steel. Once it's gone, the tank itself begins to rust, leading to leaks and discoloured water.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, leading to a more significant leak or a complete burst of the geyser tank. This could cause extensive water damage to your property, including ceilings, walls, and flooring, and will result in a complete loss of hot water.

There is a risk of electrical shorting if water comes into contact with the geyser's electrical components. For safety, it is advisable to switch off the geyser's power at your distribution board.

This image provides a close-up view of the area beneath the geyser, showing significant rust and mineral deposits on the wall and around the pipe connections. A white flexible pipe is also visible, running alongside the corroded copper pipe.
This image offers an external view of a residential building with a walkway and windows. It provides context for the location but does not show any specific fault related to the geyser.

Install a new geyser unit, ensuring all plumbing and electrical connections are correctly fitted and certified.

parsed.diagnosis: Leaking Geyser Inlet or Outlet Fitting
parsed.estimated_diagnosis_sentence: Leaking Geyser Inlet or Outlet Fitting
parsed.message:
This appears to be a leaking geyser inlet or outlet fitting. The images show water dripping from a connection on the pipework leading to the geyser, with water accumulating in the drip tray below. This type of leak typically occurs at a compression fitting, flexible braided connector, or copper joint that has begun to weep.

These leaks often develop due to thermal cycling, which can cause compression nuts to loosen over time, or when flexible connectors reach the end of their service life. Mineral deposits or limescale can sometimes build up around the affected area, indicating a long-standing drip.

If left unaddressed, a continuous leak can lead to significant water damage to ceilings, walls, or flooring beneath the geyser. It also wastes water and energy as the geyser constantly reheats the lost hot water, leading to higher utility bills.
image_descriptions: This image shows a close-up of a geyser's pipework, with a brass fitting visibly dripping water into a black plastic drip tray below. The tray contains a pool of brown, murky water, indicating a persistent leak.
This image provides a closer view of the copper pipe and its connection point, revealing dampness and dark staining on the wall directly behind the fitting, suggesting a long-term moisture issue.
This image displays an exterior view of an apartment complex with a brick wall and open windows, providing general context of the property but no specific details related to the geyser fault.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows a close-up of a geyser's pipework, with a brass fitting visibly dripping water into a black plastic drip tray below. The tray contains a pool of brown, murky water, indicating a persistent leak.
This image provides a closer view of the copper pipe and its connection point, revealing dampness and dark staining on the wall directly behind the fitting, suggesting a long-term moisture issue.
This image displays an exterior view of an apartment complex with a brick wall and open windows, providing general context of the property but no specific details related to the geyser fault.
contractor_checklist: Isolate the water supply to the geyser and drain the affected line.
Inspect all inlet and outlet fittings, including compression joints and flexible connectors, to pinpoint the exact source of the leak.
Replace the faulty fitting, connector, or section of pipework.
Test the repaired connections for leaks and restore the geyser to full operation.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Turn off the main water supply to your property and switch off the geyser's circuit breaker at the electrical distribution board.
parsed.diy_verification: Place a dry paper towel directly under the dripping fitting; if it becomes wet within an hour, the leak is confirmed.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'My geyser is leaking'.
Visible water dripping from a pipe fitting.
Water collected in a drip tray.
Dampness and staining on the wall behind the pipework.

confidence_calibration.delta_reasoning: The agent's confidence is well-calibrated. The user explicitly stated 'My geyser is leaking,' and the images clearly show water dripping from a pipe connection into a drip tray beneath a water heating unit. There is no contradictory evidence, and the visual cues directly support the user's description, making the diagnosis highly certain.

critique.notes_for_human_review: The diagnosis is accurate and well-supported by both the user's description and the visual evidence. The agent correctly identified the leaking geyser fitting and provided a clear explanation. The confidence level is appropriate for this straightforward case.

parsed.diagnosis: Broken or Missing Garage Door Spring
parsed.estimated_diagnosis_sentence: Broken or Missing Garage Door Spring
parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a broken or missing spring on one side, which is causing the door to hang unevenly and preventing proper operation. The visible detachment and bending of the lifting arm on the left side are direct consequences of this loss of counterbalance.

Garage door springs, whether torsion or extension types, are under immense tension and are designed to counterbalance the door's weight. They have a finite lifespan and typically fail due to metal fatigue from repeated opening and closing cycles. When a spring breaks, the full weight of the door is no longer supported evenly, leading to the door dropping or becoming extremely heavy.

If left unaddressed, the uneven load on the remaining components, such as the motor, cables, and tracks, can lead to further damage. The motor will strain excessively, potentially burning out, and the door itself could become permanently warped or jammed in its tracks.

Attempting to operate a garage door with a broken spring can be very dangerous. The door is extremely heavy and can fall unexpectedly, causing serious injury. It is crucial to keep clear of the door and its moving parts.
image_descriptions: This image shows the right side of the garage door, highlighting the vertical track and the attached lifting arm and bracket, which appear intact on this side.

This wider view captures the entire garage door from the inside, clearly showing it hanging crookedly with the left side significantly lower than the right, and the left lifting arm detached and bent.
A close-up of the central connecting rod from the motor to the door, showing its attachment point to the top of the door panel and the emergency release cord hanging down.
This image provides a detailed view of the bracket where the motor's connecting rod attaches to the top of the garage door, confirming this connection is still secure.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows the right side of the garage door, highlighting the vertical track and the attached lifting arm and bracket, which appear intact on this side.

This wider view captures the entire garage door from the inside, clearly showing it hanging crookedly with the left side significantly lower than the right, and the left lifting arm detached and bent.
A close-up of the central connecting rod from the motor to the door, showing its attachment point to the top of the door panel and the emergency release cord hanging down.
This image provides a detailed view of the bracket where the motor's connecting rod attaches to the top of the garage door, confirming this connection is still secure.
contractor_checklist: Inspect the entire garage door system, including both springs, cables, rollers, and tracks, to identify the exact point of failure.
Replace the broken or missing spring with a new one matched to the door's weight and specifications.
Re-tension and balance the garage door to ensure smooth and even operation.
Check and adjust the lifting cables and tracks for proper alignment and function.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Ensure the garage door motor is disengaged from the door using the emergency release cord and keep the area clear until a technician arrives.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy or drops immediately, it confirms a spring issue.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'the spring is missing on one side'.
Image 2 clearly shows the door hanging unevenly.
Left lifting arm is detached and bent, consistent with spring failure.
Motor connecting rod appears intact, ruling out motor as primary failure.

confidence_calibration.delta_reasoning: The agent's confidence is well-calibrated. The user explicitly stated the problem ('The spring is missing on one side'), and the images clearly show the garage door hanging unevenly with a detached and bent lifting arm on the left side, which directly supports the diagnosis of a broken or missing spring. There is no conflicting evidence.

critique.notes_for_human_review: The diagnosis is accurate and well-supported by both user input and visual evidence. The confidence is appropriate, and the message is comprehensive, covering the cause, progression, and safety hazards. No issues found with this diagnosis.

parsed.message:
The garage door's lifting arm on the left side has detached from its pivot point, causing the door to hang unevenly and become stuck. This detachment means the door is no longer properly supported or guided on that side.

This issue typically develops when the door's balance system (like springs or cables) is compromised, or if the door encounters an obstruction while operating. When the door is unbalanced or forced, excessive stress is placed on the lifting arm and its connection points, leading to a mechanical failure like detachment. Over time, wear and tear on the pivot pin or bracket can also contribute to this failure.

If left unattended, the uneven load on the remaining attached components can cause further damage to the garage door motor, tracks, rollers, or other lifting mechanisms. Continued attempts to operate the door in this state will exacerbate the problem and could lead to more extensive and costly repairs.

Attempting to force the door open or closed with a detached lifting arm can cause the door to fall unexpectedly, posing a significant safety hazard to anyone nearby. The door is heavy and could cause serious injury.
image_descriptions: This image provides a close-up view of the left side of the garage door, clearly showing the lifting arm detached from its pivot point on the door frame. The metal arm is hanging loose, indicating a mechanical failure.

This wider shot shows the full interior of the garage, with the wooden door hanging crookedly and lower on the left side. The ceiling-mounted motor and rail are visible, and the right lifting arm appears to be correctly attached.

This image focuses on the central ceiling-mounted rail and the red emergency release cord, showing the connection point of the motor to the door's top bracket. The detached left arm is not visible in this frame.

A close-up of the top centre bracket where the motor's drive arm connects to the garage door, showing the pin and cotter pin securing the connection. This view confirms the central attachment is intact, contrasting with the detached left arm.
image_thought_breakdown: This image provides a close-up view of the left side of the garage door, clearly showing the lifting arm detached from its pivot point on the door frame. The metal arm is hanging loose, indicating a mechanical failure.

This wider shot shows the full interior of the garage, with the wooden door hanging crookedly and lower on the left side. The ceiling-mounted motor and rail are visible, and the right lifting arm appears to be correctly attached.

This image focuses on the central ceiling-mounted rail and the red emergency release cord, showing the connection point of the motor to the door's top bracket. The detached left arm is not visible in this frame.

A close-up of the top centre bracket where the motor's drive arm connects to the garage door, showing the pin and cotter pin securing the connection. This view confirms the central attachment is intact, contrasting with the detached left arm.
contractor_checklist: Re-attach the garage door lifting arm to its pivot point.
Inspect the entire garage door system, including springs, cables, and tracks, for any underlying causes of the detachment.
Adjust and re-balance the garage door to ensure smooth and even operation.
Lubricate all moving parts and test the door's functionality with the opener.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Ensure the area around the garage door is clear of any obstructions and that the door is disengaged from the automatic opener if it is not already.
parsed.diy_verification: Carefully inspect both sides of the garage door to confirm if any other lifting arms, cables, or springs are also detached or broken.
confidence_drivers: Clear view of detached left lifting arm in Image 1
Visible tilt of the garage door in Image 2
Asymmetry between left (detached) and right (attached) lifting arms
Overall context of a garage door system

The diagnosis is specific and directly supported by the visual evidence.
